title: Smoke and dust clean-room plan
summary: A practical plan for reducing indoor smoke or dust exposure when outdoor air is poor.
content: When outdoor air is smoky or dusty, choose one room to keep cleaner. Close obvious gaps, reduce indoor burning, avoid sweeping dust into the air, and keep vulnerable people in the cleanest room. If filtration equipment exists, use it according to instructions. Do not use devices that create ozone indoors. Watch breathing comfort and seek medical help for severe symptoms.
materials: Notebook. Context: “Smoke and dust clean-room plan”.
Pencil. Context: “Smoke and dust clean-room plan”.
Labels. Context: “Smoke and dust clean-room plan”.
Clean storage space. Context: “Smoke and dust clean-room plan”.
steps: Choose a room with few gaps and low traffic.
Keep windows and doors closed when outdoor air is poor.
Avoid candles, smoking, frying, and unnecessary indoor dust.
Use damp cleaning instead of dry sweeping.
Track symptoms and official air guidance if available.
warnings: Do not block ventilation around fuel-burning appliances.
Do not ignore severe breathing trouble.
common_mistakes: Treating Smoke and dust clean-room plan as finished after the first attempt instead of writing the next review time.
Moving people or supplies for Smoke and dust clean-room plan without checking vulnerable people, exits, and safe limits first.
Mixing clean and unsafe items during Smoke and dust clean-room plan because labels, dates, or responsible names were not written down.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Smoke and dust clean-room plan with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Smoke and dust clean-room plan if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Smoke and dust clean-room plan and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Smoke and dust clean-room plan or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Smoke and dust clean-room plan are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores shelter safety, weather exposure, ventilation, dry clothing, and exit routes and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Smoke and dust clean-room plan”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Smoke and dust clean-room plan as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Smoke and dust clean-room plan when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Smoke and dust clean-room plan.
short_term: For the first hours of Smoke and dust clean-room plan, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Smoke and dust clean-room plan remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Smoke and dust clean-room plan fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Smoke and dust clean-room plan to the real environment: check shelter safety, weather exposure, ventilation, dry clothing, and exit routes, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
related_topics: Warm room and cold room shelter plan
